title: Validating multi-camera tracking against match outcomes in elite football
abstract: We analysed a full competitive season of first-team matches to test whether multi-camera tracking derived from pitch-control surface predicts match outcomes. Using data from an elite men's league, the model explained a meaningful share of variance in goal difference and was robust across opponents. The approach is directly usable by performance analysts to prepare opposition reports within the weekly training cycle.

title: A practitioner workflow for player tracking streams using high-speed running distance
abstract: This applied study describes and evaluates a workflow that turns raw high-speed running distance into coach-facing player tracking streams summaries for an elite academy-to-first-team pathway. Across 30 matches the outputs agreed with expert coach ratings and shortened analysis turnaround, supporting adoption in a professional setting.

title: A graph-neural approach to modelling optical tracking data from synchronised movement
abstract: We propose a graph-neural architecture that learns optical tracking data representations from synchronised movement. On a benchmark of simulated possessions the method improves reconstruction error over baselines. Translation to applied decision-making is not evaluated here and would require validation in a live performance environment.

title: Sensitivity of GPS-derived positional data estimates to off-ball run detection sampling frequency
abstract: This methodological paper quantifies how GPS-derived positional data estimates vary with the sampling frequency of off-ball run detection. We report substantial bias below 10 Hz. The work is primarily technical and does not test performance outcomes, but informs data-quality requirements for analytics pipelines.

title: player tracking streams in recreational five-a-side players: an exploratory study
abstract: We explored player tracking streams using high-speed running distance in a sample of recreational five-a-side players. Given the non-elite population, small pitch, and absence of a performance intervention, the findings are contextual only and unlikely to transfer to professional match preparation.
